================================================================================
  CONWAY.GLSL — COMPLETE LINE-BY-LINE EXPLANATION
================================================================================

WHAT THIS FILE IS:
  This is a GLSL fragment shader. A fragment shader runs once for EVERY SINGLE
  PIXEL on screen, in parallel on the GPU. Its sole job is to decide the color
  of one pixel. In this case, each pixel represents one cell in Conway's Game
  of Life. The shader reads the current generation from a texture, applies the
  Conway rules, and writes the next generation's cell state as a color.

WHO CALLS THIS FILE:
  The Java/Processing application loads this .glsl file, compiles it on the GPU,
  and activates it before drawing a full-screen quad. Processing's shader()
  function or raw JOGL calls bind this shader. The GPU then executes main() for
  every fragment (pixel) of that quad simultaneously.

COLOR CONVENTION:
  In the texture, BLACK (0.0) = alive, WHITE (1.0) = dead.
  Inside the shader math, we invert this so 1.0 = alive, 0.0 = dead (easier math).
  At the end, we invert back before writing to gl_FragColor.

WRAPPING:
  The grid wraps around like a torus (top connects to bottom, left to right).
  This is achieved by using fract() on texture coordinates, which wraps any
  value back into the 0.0–1.0 range.

TWO MODES:
  The file contains two completely separate implementations selected at
  compile time via a preprocessor define:
    - MULTI_STEP_K4 mode: computes 4 generations in a single shader pass
    - Default (single-step) mode: computes 1 generation per pass

================================================================================
  LINES 1–10: DECLARATIONS (shared by both modes)
================================================================================

LINE 1:  #ifdef GL_ES
  Preprocessor check. Tests if the shader is being compiled for OpenGL ES
  (mobile/embedded GPUs). Desktop OpenGL does NOT define GL_ES, so on desktop
  this entire block (lines 2–3) is skipped.

LINE 2:  precision mediump float;
  Only runs on OpenGL ES. Sets the default floating-point precision to medium
  (16-bit). Desktop OpenGL ignores precision qualifiers. This is required on
  ES or the shader won't compile.

LINE 3:  precision mediump int;
  Same as above but for integers. Sets default integer precision to medium on
  OpenGL ES devices.

LINE 4:  #endif
  Ends the #ifdef GL_ES block. Everything after this runs on all platforms.

LINE 6:  uniform sampler2D texture;
  Declares a uniform variable. "uniform" means this value is set ONCE by the
  CPU (Java side) and stays constant for every pixel in the current draw call.
  "sampler2D" is a handle to a 2D texture — this is the image containing the
  current generation of the Game of Life grid. Each pixel in this texture is
  one cell. The Java code binds the texture before drawing:
    - In Processing: shader.set("texture", currentGenPGraphics)
    - In raw JOGL:  glActiveTexture + glBindTexture + glUniform1i

LINE 7:  uniform vec2 texOffset;
  Another uniform. This is a 2-component vector (x, y) representing the size
  of one pixel in UV (texture coordinate) space. If the grid is 512×512, then
  texOffset = (1.0/512.0, 1.0/512.0). This is how we know "how far to step"
  to read a neighboring cell. Processing sets this automatically when you use
  its shader API. For raw JOGL, the Java code sets it manually via glUniform2f.

LINE 9:  varying vec4 vertColor;
  A "varying" is a value that comes FROM the vertex shader, interpolated across
  the triangle/quad. vertColor carries the vertex color. Processing's default
  vertex shader outputs this. We don't actually use it in this shader, but
  Processing expects it to be declared or the shader pipeline breaks.

LINE 10: varying vec4 vertTexCoord;
  Another varying from the vertex shader. This carries the texture coordinate
  (UV position) for the current pixel. vertTexCoord.st (or .xy) gives us the
  UV coordinate in the range [0.0, 1.0] × [0.0, 1.0] that maps this pixel to
  a position on the texture. This is THE key input — it tells us which cell
  we are currently computing.

================================================================================
  LINES 13–75: MULTI-STEP K=4 MODE
================================================================================

LINE 13: #ifdef MULTI_STEP_K4
  Preprocessor conditional. If the Java code compiled this shader with
  "#define MULTI_STEP_K4" prepended, then lines 14–75 are compiled and the
  single-step mode (lines 78–104) is excluded. The Java GPU Lab code does this
  to compute 4 generations per render pass, reducing the number of expensive
  texture read-back cycles by 4×.

--- THE conway() HELPER FUNCTION (lines 15–19) ---

LINE 15: float conway(float cell, float neighbors) {
  Declares a helper function that takes two floats:
    - cell: the current cell state (1.0 = alive, 0.0 = dead)
    - neighbors: the sum of the 8 surrounding cells (a value from 0.0 to 8.0)
  Returns 1.0 if the cell should be alive next generation, 0.0 if dead.
  This function is called from lines 42, 53, 64, and 72.

LINE 16: float survive = cell * step(2.0, neighbors) * step(neighbors, 3.0);
  Computes the SURVIVAL condition without any if-statements (branchless).
  Breaking it down piece by piece:

    step(2.0, neighbors):
      Built-in GLSL function. Returns 1.0 if neighbors >= 2.0, else 0.0.
      This checks: "does the cell have at least 2 neighbors?"

    step(neighbors, 3.0):
      Returns 1.0 if 3.0 >= neighbors (i.e., neighbors <= 3.0), else 0.0.
      This checks: "does the cell have at most 3 neighbors?"

    Multiplying them together:
      1.0 * 1.0 = 1.0 only when 2 <= neighbors <= 3 (i.e., exactly 2 or 3).
      Any other count gives 0.0.

    cell * (that result):
      If the cell is dead (0.0), survive = 0.0 regardless.
      If the cell is alive (1.0) AND has 2 or 3 neighbors, survive = 1.0.

  This implements Conway's rule: "A live cell with 2 or 3 neighbors survives."

LINE 17: float birth = (1.0 - cell) * step(3.0, neighbors) * step(neighbors, 3.0);
  Computes the BIRTH condition, also branchless.

    (1.0 - cell):
      Flips the cell state. If cell is alive (1.0), this is 0.0.
      If cell is dead (0.0), this is 1.0. Birth only applies to dead cells.

    step(3.0, neighbors):
      Returns 1.0 if neighbors >= 3.0.

    step(neighbors, 3.0):
      Returns 1.0 if neighbors <= 3.0.

    Both together: 1.0 only when neighbors == exactly 3.0.

    (1.0 - cell) * (that result):
      1.0 only when the cell is dead AND has exactly 3 neighbors.

  This implements Conway's rule: "A dead cell with exactly 3 neighbors is born."

LINE 18: return max(survive, birth);
  Returns the next state. Since survive and birth are mutually exclusive
  (a cell can't be both alive and dead), at most one of them is 1.0.
  max() picks whichever is 1.0 (or 0.0 if neither condition is met, meaning
  the cell dies from loneliness or overcrowding).

LINE 19: }
  End of the conway() helper function.

--- THE main() FUNCTION FOR MULTI-STEP MODE (lines 21–75) ---

LINE 21: void main() {
  Entry point of the fragment shader. The GPU calls this once per pixel.
  "void" means it doesn't return a value — instead it writes to gl_FragColor.

LINE 22: vec2 uv = vertTexCoord.st;
  Reads the texture coordinate for this pixel from the varying. ".st" is
  identical to ".xy" — it's just the GLSL texture coordinate naming convention.
  uv.s = horizontal position (0.0 = left, 1.0 = right)
  uv.t = vertical position (0.0 = top, 1.0 = bottom)

LINE 23: float dx = texOffset.x;
  Extracts the horizontal pixel step size. If grid is 512 wide, dx = 1/512.
  Adding dx to uv.x moves one cell to the right.

LINE 24: float dy = texOffset.y;
  Extracts the vertical pixel step size. Adding dy to uv.y moves one cell down.

--- STEP 0: Read the 9×9 neighborhood from the texture (lines 26–33) ---

  WHY 9×9? In multi-step mode we compute 4 generations at once. Each generation's
  Conway rule looks at a 3×3 neighborhood (the cell + 8 neighbors). After 4
  generations, the "influence radius" expands to 4 cells in every direction from
  center. So we need a 9×9 block of the ORIGINAL state (generation 0) to have
  enough data to cascade through 4 generations and get the correct answer for
  the center cell.

LINE 26: float g0[81];
  Declares an array of 81 floats (9 × 9 = 81) to store generation 0 cell states.
  This is a local variable — each pixel gets its own copy on the GPU.

LINE 27: for (int row = -4; row <= 4; row++) {
  Outer loop: iterates over 9 rows, from 4 above center to 4 below center.
  row = -4, -3, -2, -1, 0, +1, +2, +3, +4  →  9 values.

LINE 28: for (int col = -4; col <= 4; col++) {
  Inner loop: iterates over 9 columns per row.
  col = -4 through +4  →  9 values.
  Total iterations: 9 × 9 = 81 texture fetches.

LINE 29: vec2 offset = vec2(float(col) * dx, float(row) * dy);
  Computes the UV offset from the center pixel to the (row, col) neighbor.
  float(col) casts the int to float (GLSL requires explicit cast).
  Multiplying by dx/dy converts from "cell count" to UV space.
  Example: col=1, dx=1/512 → offset.x = 1/512 (one cell to the right).

LINE 30: float texVal = texture2D(texture, fract(uv + offset)).r;
  THIS IS THE CORE TEXTURE READ. Breaking it down:

    uv + offset:
      The UV coordinate of the neighbor cell.

    fract(uv + offset):
      fract() returns the fractional part (e.g., fract(1.2) = 0.2, fract(-0.1) = 0.9).
      This implements TOROIDAL WRAPPING. If uv + offset goes past 1.0 (right/bottom
      edge), fract wraps it back to the left/top. If it goes below 0.0, fract wraps
      to the opposite edge.

    texture2D(texture, ...):
      Built-in GLSL function. Reads a color from the texture at the given UV coordinate.
      Returns a vec4 (RGBA). The texture is the current generation image.

    .r:
      Takes only the red channel. Since cells are grayscale (black or white),
      R = G = B, so we only need one channel. Returns 0.0 for black (alive)
      or 1.0 for white (dead).

LINE 31: g0[(row + 4) * 9 + (col + 4)] = 1.0 - texVal;
  Stores the cell state into the array, INVERTING the color convention:
    (row + 4): shifts row from [-4,+4] to [0,8] for array indexing.
    * 9: each row has 9 columns.
    (col + 4): shifts col from [-4,+4] to [0,8].
    So the array index goes from 0 to 80.
    
    1.0 - texVal: inverts the value. Texture has 0.0=alive, but our math uses
    1.0=alive. So: 1.0 - 0.0 = 1.0 (alive), 1.0 - 1.0 = 0.0 (dead).

LINE 32: }   (closes inner for loop)
LINE 33: }   (closes outer for loop)

--- STEP 1: Generation 1 — Apply Conway to 7×7 from g0 → g1 (lines 35–44) ---

  After reading the 9×9 block (g0), we now have enough data to compute
  generation 1 for the inner 7×7 cells. Why 7×7? Each Conway step needs a
  1-cell border around it for neighbor lookups. 9×9 minus a 1-cell border on
  each side = 7×7.

LINE 35: float g1[49];
  Array for generation 1 results. 7 × 7 = 49 cells.

LINE 36: for (int r = 1; r <= 7; r++) {
  Iterates rows 1 through 7 of the g0 array (skipping the outer border).

LINE 37: for (int c = 1; c <= 7; c++) {
  Iterates columns 1 through 7 of g0.

LINE 38: float cell = g0[r * 9 + c];
  Reads the current cell state from generation 0. r * 9 + c indexes into
  the 9-wide g0 array at position (row=r, col=c).

LINES 39–41:
  float n = g0[(r-1)*9+(c-1)] + g0[(r-1)*9+c] + g0[(r-1)*9+(c+1)]
          + g0[ r   *9+(c-1)]                  + g0[ r   *9+(c+1)]
          + g0[(r+1)*9+(c-1)] + g0[(r+1)*9+c] + g0[(r+1)*9+(c+1)];

  Sums the 8 neighbors around (r, c) in the g0 array:
    Row above (r-1): top-left, top-center, top-right
    Same row (r):    left, [skip center], right
    Row below (r+1): bottom-left, bottom-center, bottom-right
  The result n is a float from 0.0 to 8.0.

LINE 42: g1[(r - 1) * 7 + (c - 1)] = conway(cell, n);
  Calls the conway() helper (defined on line 15) to determine the next state.
  Stores the result in g1 with shifted indices: (r-1) and (c-1) because g1
  starts at index 0 for what was row 1, col 1 in g0. g1 is 7 columns wide.

LINE 43: }   (closes inner loop)
LINE 44: }   (closes outer loop)

--- STEP 2: Generation 2 — Apply Conway to 5×5 from g1 → g2 (lines 46–55) ---

  Same pattern. g1 is 7×7, and we lose a 1-cell border → inner 5×5.

LINE 46: float g2[25];
  Array for generation 2. 5 × 5 = 25 cells.

LINES 47–54:
  Identical structure to Step 1. Loops r=1..5, c=1..5 through g1 (which is
  7 columns wide, hence *7 in the index math). Each cell reads its 8 neighbors
  from g1, calls conway(), and stores the result in g2 (5 columns wide, hence
  (r-1)*5+(c-1) indexing).

LINE 55: }   (closes outer loop)

--- STEP 3: Generation 3 — Apply Conway to 3×3 from g2 → g3 (lines 57–66) ---

  g2 is 5×5, lose border → inner 3×3.

LINE 57: float g3[9];
  Array for generation 3. 3 × 3 = 9 cells.

LINES 58–65:
  Same pattern. Loops r=1..3, c=1..3 through g2 (5 columns wide, *5).
  Stores results in g3 (3 columns wide, *3).

--- STEP 4: Generation 4 — Final Conway on center cell (lines 68–72) ---

  g3 is 3×3. One more Conway step gives us the single center cell result.

LINE 68: float centerCell = g3[4];
  g3[4] is the center of the 3×3 grid:
    [0][1][2]
    [3][4][5]    ← index 4 is the center
    [6][7][8]

LINES 69–71:
  float neighbors = g3[0] + g3[1] + g3[2]
                   + g3[3]          + g3[5]
                   + g3[6] + g3[7] + g3[8];
  Sums all 8 cells around the center (skipping index 4).

LINE 72: float result = conway(centerCell, neighbors);
  Applies Conway rules one final time. result is the state of this pixel
  after 4 complete generations.

LINE 74: gl_FragColor = vec4(vec3(1.0 - result), 1.0);
  WRITES THE FINAL OUTPUT COLOR.

    1.0 - result:
      Inverts back to texture convention (1.0=dead=white, 0.0=alive=black).

    vec3(1.0 - result):
      Creates (R, G, B) all set to the same value → grayscale.

    vec4(..., 1.0):
      Adds alpha = 1.0 (fully opaque).

    gl_FragColor:
      Built-in GLSL output variable. Whatever vec4 you write here becomes the
      color of this pixel in the output framebuffer/texture. The GPU collects
      all pixels' gl_FragColor values to form the complete next-generation image.

LINE 75: }
  End of multi-step main().

================================================================================
  LINES 78–104: SINGLE-STEP MODE (1 generation per pass)
================================================================================

LINE 78: #else
  Preprocessor else-branch. If MULTI_STEP_K4 was NOT defined at compile time,
  the compiler uses this simpler single-step version instead. This is used by
  the live simulation via Processing's shader API.

LINE 80: void main() {
  Entry point for single-step mode. Same purpose as line 21.

LINE 81: vec2 uv = vertTexCoord.st;
  Same as line 22. Gets this pixel's texture coordinate.

LINE 82: float dx = texOffset.x;
  Same as line 23. Horizontal step size.

LINE 83: float dy = texOffset.y;
  Same as line 24. Vertical step size.

LINE 85: float n = 0.0;
  Initializes the neighbor counter to zero.

LINES 86–93: Count all 8 neighbors.
  Each line reads one neighbor cell and adds its value to n.

LINE 86: n += 1.0 - texture2D(texture, fract(uv + vec2(-dx, -dy))).r;
  TOP-LEFT neighbor.
    vec2(-dx, -dy): offset is one cell left and one cell up.
    uv + vec2(-dx, -dy): UV position of the top-left neighbor.
    fract(...): wraps for toroidal topology.
    texture2D(texture, ...): reads the cell color from the texture.
    .r: red channel (0.0 = black/alive in texture, 1.0 = white/dead).
    1.0 - (...): inverts so 1.0 = alive, 0.0 = dead.
    n += ...: adds to neighbor count. A living neighbor contributes 1.0.

LINE 87: n += 1.0 - texture2D(texture, fract(uv + vec2(0.0, -dy))).r;
  TOP-CENTER neighbor. Same X, one cell up.

LINE 88: n += 1.0 - texture2D(texture, fract(uv + vec2( dx, -dy))).r;
  TOP-RIGHT neighbor. One cell right, one cell up.

LINE 89: n += 1.0 - texture2D(texture, fract(uv + vec2(-dx, 0.0))).r;
  LEFT neighbor. One cell left, same row.

LINE 90: n += 1.0 - texture2D(texture, fract(uv + vec2( dx, 0.0))).r;
  RIGHT neighbor. One cell right, same row.

LINE 91: n += 1.0 - texture2D(texture, fract(uv + vec2(-dx,  dy))).r;
  BOTTOM-LEFT neighbor. One cell left, one cell down.

LINE 92: n += 1.0 - texture2D(texture, fract(uv + vec2(0.0,  dy))).r;
  BOTTOM-CENTER neighbor. Same X, one cell down.

LINE 93: n += 1.0 - texture2D(texture, fract(uv + vec2( dx,  dy))).r;
  BOTTOM-RIGHT neighbor. One cell right, one cell down.

  After all 8 lines, n contains the total number of alive neighbors (0.0 to 8.0).

LINE 95: float cell = 1.0 - texture2D(texture, uv).r;
  Reads THIS pixel's own cell state (no offset, just uv).
  Inverts: 1.0 = alive, 0.0 = dead.

LINE 97: float survive = cell * step(2.0, n) * step(n, 3.0);
  Identical logic to line 16 in the conway() function.
  survive = 1.0 only if cell is alive AND n is 2 or 3.

LINE 98: float birth = (1.0 - cell) * step(3.0, n) * step(n, 3.0);
  Identical logic to line 17.
  birth = 1.0 only if cell is dead AND n is exactly 3.

LINE 99: float next = max(survive, birth);
  Identical to line 18. next = 1.0 if the cell lives, 0.0 if it dies.

LINE 101: gl_FragColor = vec4(vec3(1.0 - next), 1.0);
  Identical to line 74. Inverts back to texture convention and writes the pixel.

LINE 102: }
  End of single-step main().

LINE 104: #endif
  Ends the #ifdef/#else/#endif preprocessor block. The compiler only ever
  compiles ONE of the two modes — whichever the Java code selected.

LINE 105: (empty trailing newline)
  Standard practice. Many compilers/tools expect files to end with a newline.

================================================================================
  EXECUTION FLOW SUMMARY
================================================================================

  1. Java application initializes a texture containing the grid (black/white pixels).
  2. Java binds the texture and sets uniforms (texture, texOffset).
  3. Java draws a full-screen quad with this shader active.
  4. GPU launches main() for EVERY pixel simultaneously (massively parallel).
  5. Each pixel:
     a. Reads its UV position from vertTexCoord
     b. Samples neighbor cells from the texture
     c. Applies Conway rules (branchless math)
     d. Writes the next-gen state to gl_FragColor
  6. The GPU assembles all pixel outputs into a new texture.
  7. Java swaps textures (new output becomes next input) and repeats.

  In SINGLE-STEP mode: each draw call = 1 generation.
  In MULTI-STEP mode:  each draw call = 4 generations.

================================================================================
  KEY GLSL BUILT-IN FUNCTIONS USED
================================================================================

  texture2D(sampler, uv):  Reads a color (vec4) from a texture at UV position.
  fract(x):                Returns the fractional part: x - floor(x).
  step(edge, x):           Returns 0.0 if x < edge, else 1.0. No branching.
  max(a, b):               Returns the larger of a and b.
  vec2(x, y):              Constructs a 2D vector.
  vec3(x):                 Constructs a 3D vector with all components = x.
  vec4(rgb, a):            Constructs a 4D vector (color with alpha).

================================================================================
  KEY GLSL CONCEPTS IN THIS SHADER
================================================================================

  uniform:   Set once per draw call by CPU. Same for all pixels.
  varying:   Interpolated per-pixel from vertex shader outputs.
  sampler2D: A reference to a bound texture.
  gl_FragColor: The output — the color this pixel will be drawn as.
  #ifdef:    Compile-time conditional (preprocessor). Not runtime branching.
  step():    The "branchless trick" — avoids GPU-costly if/else statements.
             GPUs run pixels in lockstep (warps/wavefronts), so branches where
             some pixels take one path and others take another path cause both
             paths to execute. step() avoids this entirely with pure math.
